title: Class templates

canonicalExplanation.summary: A class template defines a family of class types whose members and invariants depend on template arguments, with each specialization being a distinct type.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Box<int> and Box<std::string> share a template pattern but are distinct types with independently instantiated operations. Your goal is to implement and test a small class template with clear value semantics. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Standard algorithms; Class interface and invariants; Function templates; Copy and assignment semantics. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A class template defines a family of class types whose members and invariants depend on template arguments, with each specialization being a distinct type. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Member definitions need appropriate template syntax, incomplete instantiation can defer errors, deduction guides affect construction, and static members are per specialization. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: All specializations of a class template are implicitly convertible because they come from the same template.

includes: Parameterize a small value or container-like type without duplicating implementation.
Implement and test a small class template with clear value semantics.
excludes: Member definitions need appropriate template syntax, incomplete instantiation can defer errors, deduction guides affect construction, and static members are per specialization.

outcomes: Implement and test a small class template with clear value semantics.

examples.title: Class templates: defined case
examples.situation: Box<int> and Box<std::string> share a template pattern but are distinct types with independently instantiated operations.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: Box<int> and Box<std::string> share a template pattern but are distinct types with independently instantiated operations.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Each class-template specialization is a distinct type; conversions exist only when explicitly declared or otherwise provided.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Each class-template specialization is a distinct type; conversions exist only when explicitly declared or otherwise provided.

examples.title: Class templates: boundary case
examples.situation: A member requiring ordering is constrained or instantiated only when the element type supports the required comparison.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A member requiring ordering is constrained or instantiated only when the element type supports the required comparison.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Member definitions need appropriate template syntax, incomplete instantiation can defer errors, deduction guides affect construction, and static members are per specialization.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: All specializations of a class template are implicitly convertible because they come from the same template.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and test a small class template with clear value semantics.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: All specializations of a class template are implicitly convertible because they come from the same template.
misconceptions.correction: Each class-template specialization is a distinct type; conversions exist only when explicitly declared or otherwise provided.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: All specializations of a class template are implicitly convertible because they come from the same template.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Class templates: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Member definitions need appropriate template syntax, incomplete instantiation can defer errors, deduction guides affect construction, and static members are per specialization.
